major_pests.name: Alfalfa weevil
major_pests.life_stage_note: Larvae and adults defoliate terminals and first regrowth
major_pests.note: Peak damage at early spring growth in temperate and continental systems.

major_pests.name: Potato leafhopper
major_pests.note: V-shaped yellowing and stunted regrowth; chronic in warm dry and tropical irrigated fields.

major_pests.name: Aphids
major_pests.life_stage_note: Colonies build on new regrowth after cutting
major_pests.note: Pea, spotted, and blue aphids reduce vigor and can vector viruses.

major_pests.name: Caterpillar defoliators
major_pests.note: Armyworm, alfalfa caterpillar, and loopers often flare after cutting on tender regrowth.

major_pests.name: Root-feeding insects
major_pests.life_stage_note: Larval feeding on roots and crowns
major_pests.note: Clover root curculio, white grubs, and wireworms cause cumulative damage often misdiagnosed as disease.

major_pests.name: Blister beetles
major_pests.note: Food-safety and livestock toxicity concern rather than stand-loss pest.

major_pests.name: Anthracnose
major_pests.note: Crown and stem lesions; worse in warm, humid regrowth periods.

major_pests.name: Bacterial wilt
major_pests.note: Systemic wilt favored by wounds and warm soils.

major_pests.name: Common and spring black stem
major_pests.note: Stem and leaf lesions reduce quality and regrowth.

major_pests.name: Crown and root rots
major_pests.note: Phytophthora, Aphanomyces, Rhizoctonia, and Fusarium; linked to poor drainage, compaction, and winter injury.

major_pests.name: Sclerotinia crown and stem rot
major_pests.note: White mold under cool, humid canopy conditions.

major_pests.name: Nematodes
major_pests.note: Root-knot, cyst, and lesion nematodes cause root dysfunction and stress symptoms.

biological_controls.name: Lady beetles, lacewings, minute pirate bugs, damsel bugs
biological_controls.note: Generalist predators suppress soft-bodied pests; conserve field margins and avoid broad-spectrum sprays during peak activity.

biological_controls.name: Parasitoid wasps
biological_controls.note: Important against aphids and caterpillars when habitat support starts early.

biological_controls.name: Spiders
biological_controls.note: Reduce caterpillars and other surface-active pests in undisturbed sward margins.

biological_controls.name: Cutting height and timing
biological_controls.note: Maintain 5-7 cm residual height; cut before peak weevil larvae and severe hopperburn thresholds on seedling regrowth.

biological_controls.name: Dormancy and disease resistance selection
biological_controls.note: Match fall dormancy to climate; use resistance to anthracnose and bacterial wilt.

biological_controls.name: Rotation and field sanitation
biological_controls.note: Rotate out of alfalfa for 2-4 years; avoid direct re-seeding on old alfalfa ground.

biological_controls.name: Grazing and traffic management
biological_controls.note: Limit pugging, wheel ruts, and overgrazing that wound crowns and open rot entry points.

biological_controls.name: Drainage and compaction management
biological_controls.note: Improve drainage and reduce compaction on wet or poorly structured sites.

biological_controls.name: Bt-based materials
biological_controls.note: For caterpillar flares where labels and conditions allow.

biological_controls.name: Entomopathogenic nematodes
biological_controls.note: Supplement soil-dwelling larval control where labels and soil moisture allow.

chemical_controls.note: Treat when larval counts exceed local thresholds before first cut; rotate MOA groups seasonally.
Treat when scouting confirms nymph populations threatening stand vigor; avoid IRAC 4A during bloom.
Treat when regrowth colonization exceeds threshold; prefer selective MOA to conserve beneficials.
Defensible in seed or dehydration production or when severe disease threatens stand survival; rotate FRAC groups between cutting cycles.
Threshold-based use only; observe PHI for hay and silage; zero tolerance for off-label residues in forage channels.
Avoid broad-spectrum sprays during bloom to protect pollinators and seed-set in seed fields.

metric_definitions.first_cut_days: Days from seeding to first cut
metric_definitions.cut_interval_days: Typical interval between cuts in peak season

maturity_profile.localization_note: Ranges are broad; localize by variety, dormancy class, irrigation level, and cutting management.

tropical.note: Irrigated production only; heat and pest turnover shorten stand life without careful water and drainage management.

dry.note: Core zone when irrigated; rain-fed alfalfa marginal except where seasonal moisture supports establishment.

temperate.note: Primary alfalfa region; seasonal pest windows with strong multi-cut yield.

continental.note: Winter hardiness and crown rot are primary stand-life limits.

high_mountain.note: Cool short-season niche; dormancy class and drainage critical.

polar.note: Long-duration lucerne unreliable in open-field polar margins; treat as out-of-scope except protected microclimate exceptions.

tropical.pest_pressure: Chronic leafhopper and aphid pressure under irrigation.
tropical.disease_profile: Crown and root rots increase where drainage is poor or irrigation is excessive.
tropical.development: High regrowth potential but shorter stand life without water and drainage management.

dry.pest_pressure: Leafhopper pressure seasonal and threshold-driven when irrigated.
dry.disease_profile: Root and crown stress linked to moisture extremes and compaction.
dry.development: Strong yield under irrigation; rain-fed production generally marginal.

temperate.pest_pressure: Alfalfa weevil and seasonal leafhopper windows.
temperate.disease_profile: Foliar disease increases with humidity; crown rot on wet sites.
temperate.development: Defined cutting windows with 3-5 cuts per year typical.

continental.pest_pressure: Similar to temperate with sharper winter-injury interaction.
continental.disease_profile: Winter injury and crown or root rot are stand-life risks.
continental.development: Fewer cuts per year; stand life 4-6 years with good crown management.

high_mountain.pest_pressure: Lower pest turnover; fewer insect flares per season.
high_mountain.disease_profile: Winter injury and drainage-linked rots dominate.
high_mountain.development: Longer intervals between cuts; 2-3 cuts per year typical.

polar.development: Perennial alfalfa not a dependable crop class in open polar margins.

major_pests.name: Aphids
major_pests.life_stage_note: Colonies on young tissue and post-cut regrowth
major_pests.note: Reduce vigor and vector viruses; clover, pea, and spotted aphid complexes.

major_pests.name: Leafhoppers
major_pests.life_stage_note: Nymphs and adults on warm regrowth
major_pests.note: Hopperburn and virus transmission in warm regrowth periods.

major_pests.name: Caterpillar defoliators
major_pests.note: Armyworm, loopers, and webworms; worst in pure stands and new seedlings.

major_pests.name: Root weevils
major_pests.life_stage_note: Adults notch leaves; larvae feed root nodules
major_pests.note: Clover root curculio and Sitona weevil reduce N fixation.

major_pests.name: Clover head weevil
major_pests.life_stage_note: Adults and larvae in seed heads
major_pests.note: Primary concern in clover seed production.

major_pests.name: Thrips
major_pests.note: Seed crop concern affecting flower quality.

major_pests.name: Powdery mildew, rust, and leaf spot complexes
major_pests.note: Foliar quality loss; worse in humid temperate and tropical conditions.

major_pests.name: Sclerotinia stem and crown rot
major_pests.note: Devastating in dense pure stands under cool, wet canopy conditions.

major_pests.name: Root and crown rots
major_pests.note: Phytophthora, Rhizoctonia, and Fusarium linked to waterlogging and compaction.

major_pests.name: Virus complexes
major_pests.note: Alfalfa mosaic, bean yellow mosaic, and clover yellow vein; aphid-vectored in seed fields.

biological_controls.name: Lady beetles, lacewings, parasitoid wasps, predatory bugs
biological_controls.note: Clover-grass mixtures often support higher beneficial diversity than pure clover stands.

biological_controls.name: Clover-grass mixture design
biological_controls.note: Mixed swards dilute pest impact and improve sward stability across grazing and hay systems.

biological_controls.name: Rotation and fallow
biological_controls.note: Avoid consecutive pure-clover crops on the same ground to cut disease inoculum and root-pest carryover.

biological_controls.name: Grazing and cutting strategy
biological_controls.note: Remove inoculum-laden tissue before dense humid canopy develops; protect stolons and crowns from overgrazing.

biological_controls.name: Drainage and compaction management
biological_controls.note: Prevent waterlogging over white clover stolons and red clover crowns; limit traffic on wet soils.

biological_controls.name: Species and resistance selection
biological_controls.note: Match red, white, subterranean, or annual clover type to rainfall and temperature; use rust- and mildew-resistant varieties where available.

chemical_controls.note: Treat when scouting confirms threshold on regrowth; prefer selective MOA over broad-spectrum pyrethroids in mixed pastures.
Treat larval flares on seedlings or pure stands with seasonal MOA rotation.
Defensible in seed production or high-value hay; rarely economical in routine grazing pasture.
Avoid unnecessary soil-applied broad-spectrum treatments at sowing to preserve N-fixing symbioses.
Observe PHI for hay and silage; rotate MOA groups seasonally.

metric_definitions.establishment_days: Days from sowing to usable grazing or first cut
metric_definitions.stand_life_years: Expected productive stand duration by species

maturity_profile.localization_note: Ranges vary by clover species (red, white, subterranean, annual); localize by type and management.

subterranean_clover.note: Autumn germination and spring use; annual reseeding cycle if burrs are allowed to set.

tropical.note: Cool-season clovers marginal in hot lowlands; heat-tolerant annual types under irrigation only.

dry.note: Berseem and irrigated annual clovers fit; subterranean clover suits Mediterranean winter-rainfall patterns only.

temperate.note: Primary clover zone; red and white clover standard in mixed swards.

continental.note: Similar to temperate; red clover persistence may shorten under harsher winters.

high_mountain.note: Red and white clover perform in cool moist sites; shorter seasons limit biomass.

polar.note: Fringe cool-season niche only; not a primary forage class.

tropical.pest_pressure: Aphid and virus pressure on irrigated annual types.
tropical.development: White clover poor persistence in hot lowlands.

dry.pest_pressure: Seasonal aphid flares on irrigated stands.
dry.disease_profile: Root injury on poorly drained irrigated sites.
dry.development: Annual and Mediterranean types fit best; perennials shorten in persistence.

temperate.pest_pressure: Aphids, leafhoppers, and defoliators on regrowth.
temperate.disease_profile: Rust, mildew, and Sclerotinia in humid dense stands.
temperate.development: Red and white clover are standard pasture components.

continental.pest_pressure: Similar to temperate with sharper winter stress.
continental.disease_profile: Crown and root rots on wet compacted sites.
continental.development: Strong fit for mixed hay and grazing systems.

high_mountain.disease_profile: Sclerotinia risk in cool wet canopies.
high_mountain.development: Good persistence possible but fewer cuts per season.

major_pests.name: Stem borers
major_pests.note: European corn borer and tropical borer species reduce standability and open stalk rot entry points.

major_pests.name: Fall armyworm and true armyworm
major_pests.note: Can destroy stand in seedling and vegetative stages.

major_pests.name: Cutworms
major_pests.life_stage_note: Nocturnal larvae cut seedlings at soil line
major_pests.note: Establishment pest causing stand gaps.

major_pests.name: Corn earworm
major_pests.note: Affects grain portion of silage dry matter and mycotoxin risk.

major_pests.name: Sap feeders
major_pests.life_stage_note: Aphids, thrips, and leafhoppers on foliage
major_pests.note: Secondary unless populations are extreme.

major_pests.name: Rootworms
major_pests.note: Where present, causes pre-harvest lodging.

major_pests.name: Wireworms and white grubs
major_pests.note: Cause stand gaps and uneven emergence.

major_pests.name: Foliar blights
major_pests.note: Northern corn leaf blight, gray leaf spot, and anthracnose leaf blight reduce leaf area and silage quality.

major_pests.name: Rusts
major_pests.note: Common and southern rust can defoliate susceptible hybrids rapidly under warm humid conditions.

major_pests.name: Stalk rots
major_pests.note: Fusarium, Colletotrichum, and Stenocarpella cause lodging and mycotoxin risk; often follow borer injury or drought stress.

major_pests.name: Root rots and seedling blights
major_pests.note: Pythium and Rhizoctonia cause stand gaps at emergence.

biological_controls.name: Host-plant resistance traits
biological_controls.note: Select hybrids with appropriate borer, rootworm, and foliar disease traits for region and season length.

biological_controls.name: Trichogramma wasps
biological_controls.note: Egg parasitism against stem borer complexes where releases or habitat support are feasible.

biological_controls.name: Bt-based bioinsecticides
biological_controls.note: For caterpillar control where Bt traits are absent or as supplement in threshold-driven programs.

biological_controls.name: Entomopathogenic nematodes
biological_controls.note: Supplement cutworm and grub suppression at establishment where labels allow.

biological_controls.name: Rotation and residue management
biological_controls.note: Break continuous maize; manage residue to reduce borer overwintering and disease inoculum while controlling erosion.

biological_controls.name: Plant density and hybrid maturity matching
biological_controls.note: Match maturity class to season length; avoid over-density that increases stalk rot and lodging.

biological_controls.name: Harvest timing at target dry matter
biological_controls.note: Chop at 30-38% DM (milk to early dent) to balance fiber digestibility, starch, and disease or lodging risk.

chemical_controls.note: Neonicotinoid plus fungicide seed treatments protect emergence against soil insects and seedling diseases.
Treat at establishment and whorl stage when scouting confirms threshold; rotate MOA within season.
Pre-tassel and reproductive treatment when trap or scouting thresholds are met.
Defensible on susceptible hybrids when foliar disease threatens leaf area before dent stage; stalk rot management is primarily hybrid selection and borer control.
Whole-plant harvest requires PHI compliance before chop; monitor drought-stressed or borer-damaged fields for mycotoxin risk.

metric_definitions.days_to_optimal_harvest: Days from planting to whole-plant chop at target 30-38% DM

maturity_profile.localization_note: Ranges by hybrid maturity class (early, medium, late); localize by relative maturity and frost window.

tropical.note: Faster calendar cycle but higher mid-season borer and armyworm pressure.

dry.note: Similar to tropical when irrigated; drought stress before dent increases stalk rot and lodging risk.

temperate.note: Frost is the hard season stop; foliar disease and lodging are late-season concerns.

continental.note: Longer duration; late-season stalk rot and lodging are primary harvest-timing concerns.

high_mountain.note: Ultra-short and short-season hybrids only; late types not recommended.

polar.note: Not viable except niche protected sites.

tropical.pest_pressure: High stem borer, armyworm, and stalk pest pressure.
tropical.disease_profile: Rust and stalk rot under warm humid conditions.
tropical.development: Shorter calendar duration with continuous pest risk.

dry.pest_pressure: Armyworm and borer flares in warm irrigated seasons.
dry.disease_profile: Stalk rot increases with drought stress before dent.
dry.development: Irrigation timing critical for biomass accumulation.

temperate.pest_pressure: Cutworm at establishment; borer where present.
temperate.disease_profile: Foliar blights and rust on susceptible hybrids.
temperate.development: Longer season with frost as termination event.

continental.pest_pressure: Similar to temperate with lodging risk at harvest.
continental.development: Late hybrids need full warm-season window.

high_mountain.disease_profile: Compressed disease window but frost limits recovery.
high_mountain.development: Short-season hybrids with slow cool-period development.

polar.development: Standard silage maize not viable in open polar margins.

major_pests.name: Cutworms
major_pests.life_stage_note: Larval seedling cutting at soil line
major_pests.note: Establishment pest in cool-season grass sowings.

major_pests.name: Armyworms
major_pests.note: Rapid flares on lush spring or autumn growth in cool-season and warm-season grasses.

major_pests.name: Billbugs
major_pests.life_stage_note: Larval stem and crown feeding; adult leaf notching
major_pests.note: Primary ryegrass concern.

major_pests.name: Aphids and chinch bug
major_pests.note: Reduce vigor especially under drought or heat stress.

major_pests.name: Leatherjackets
major_pests.note: Temperate pasture concern in some regions.

major_pests.name: Spittlebugs
major_pests.note: Honeydew and sooty mold reduce bermuda palatability and vigor.

major_pests.name: Bermuda grass mites
major_pests.note: Stunted growth and reduced sward quality on bermuda.

major_pests.name: White grubs and mole crickets
major_pests.note: Warm-season grass establishment and persistence concern.

major_pests.name: Rust, powdery mildew, leaf spot, red thread
major_pests.note: Foliar quality loss; worse in humid temperate and tropical conditions.

major_pests.name: Snow mold
major_pests.note: Winter injury and foliar kill in temperate zones.

major_pests.name: Crown and root rots
major_pests.note: Linked to wet, compacted soils across grass types.

major_pests.name: Leaf spot, rust, dollar spot, spring dead spot
major_pests.note: Warm-season foliar and crown disease reducing sward density.

major_pests.name: Helminthosporium leaf blight
major_pests.note: Defoliation under warm humid conditions on warm-season grasses.

biological_controls.name: Ground beetles, rove beetles, parasitoid wasps, predatory mites
biological_controls.note: Conserved by minimizing broad-spectrum sprays and maintaining field margins.

biological_controls.name: Grazing and mowing management
biological_controls.note: Avoid overgrazing below species-specific residual height; rotate grazing pressure to allow recovery.

biological_controls.name: Species and variety selection
biological_controls.note: Match cool-season (ryegrass, fescue) or warm-season (bermuda, bahia, Rhodes) types to site, drainage, and end-use.

biological_controls.name: Endophyte-enhanced grasses
biological_controls.note: Fescue and ryegrass endophytes confer insect resistance; manage ergot alkaloid livestock safety.

biological_controls.name: Mixed legume-grass swards
biological_controls.note: Improve diversity and reduce pure-stand pest flares.

biological_controls.name: Soil fertility and drainage
biological_controls.note: Balanced N prevents lush disease-prone growth; lime and drainage reduce red thread and pH-related disease.

chemical_controls.note: Treat when larval counts exceed threshold; prefer selective MOA over blanket pyrethroid across large pasture areas.
Treat when honeydew, sooty mold, and vigor loss exceed local threshold; spot-treat where possible.
Defensible in seed production or high-value hay; rarely cost-effective on grazing pasture.
Selective weed control maintains sward density; rotate HRAC groups and avoid damage to legume components in mixed pastures.
Use economic thresholds, spot-treat where possible, and avoid unnecessary broad-spectrum sprays across large pasture areas.

metric_definitions.establishment_days: Days from sowing or planting to first graze or cut
metric_definitions.regrowth_interval_days: Typical interval between grazings or cuts in peak season

maturity_profile.localization_note: Ranges by grass species and functional type (cool_season vs warm_season); localize by management intensity.

tropical.note: Warm-season grasses dominate; year-round grazing possible with irrigation; continuous pest pressure.

dry.note: Warm-season grasses persist with drought tolerance; cool-season grasses seasonal; regrowth slows in dry periods.

temperate.note: Cool-season grasses peak; warm-season types summer-active in transition zones.

continental.note: Cool-season dominance; summer stress may shorten ryegrass persistence.

high_mountain.note: Slow cool-period development; long regrowth intervals.

polar.note: Very short cool-season niche; limited advisory scope.

tropical.pest_pressure: Armyworm and spittlebug pressure on warm-season grasses; continuous pest risk.
tropical.disease_profile: Rust and leaf spot under humid conditions.
tropical.development: Warm-season grasses dominate; year-round grazing with irrigation.

dry.disease_profile: Lower foliar disease than humid zones; stress disorders dominate.
dry.development: Warm-season persistence; cool-season grasses seasonal only.

temperate.pest_pressure: Billbugs, armyworms, and aphids on cool-season swards.
temperate.disease_profile: Rust, red thread, and snow mold in humid seasons.
temperate.development: Cool-season grasses primary; strong spring and autumn growth.

continental.pest_pressure: Similar to temperate with sharper summer stress.
continental.disease_profile: Crown and root rots on wet compacted sites.
continental.development: Fescue persistence often longer than ryegrass.

high_mountain.disease_profile: Snow mold and winter injury risks.
high_mountain.development: Cool-season types with slow establishment and long regrowth intervals.

polar.development: Very short season; not a primary pasture class.
